1: vayetze hagoral libnei Yosef (goral, H1486; bnei Yosef, H3130): 'the lot went out for the children of Joseph.' Joseph's two sons — Ephraim and Manasseh — together constitute the largest tribal territory in the central highlands. The singular 'lot for the children of Joseph' (before the separate Ephraim/Manasseh descriptions) reflects the administrative unity of the Joseph tribes: they received a common territory that was subsequently divided between the two half-tribes. Min haYarden biriycho (from the Jordan at Jericho) — the territory begins at the Jordan crossing site, the most sacred geographical location of the conquest narrative.
2: veyatzva miBeitel Luz: 'it goes out from Bethel to Luz.' Bethel and Luz are identified as the same place (Genesis 28:19: 'he called the name of that place Bethel, but the name of the city was Luz at first'). The pre-Israelite name Luz (nut-tree or almond-tree) was replaced by the Israelite name Bethel (House of God) after Jacob's theophany. The boundary's passage through Bethel is significant: Bethel was both the site of the patriarchal promise (Genesis 28:10-22) and, after the division of the kingdom, the site of Jeroboam's golden calves (1 Kings 12:28-29). The sacred site that anchored the promise to Jacob became the site of the northern kingdom's idolatry.
3: veyarad Yama el-gevul haYafleiti: 'it goes down westward to the border of the Japhletites.' The southern boundary of the Joseph territory descends westward from the central highland to the Shephelah and coastal plain. The naming of boundary groups ('the border of the Japhletites') reflects the ethnic geography of the pre-conquest period — populations whose names are preserved only in boundary descriptions like this one. The specificity of these ethnic boundary markers suggests the antiquity of the boundary traditions: later redactors would not have invented obscure group names, but preservers of ancient records would have copied what they received.
4: vayinchalu bnei Yosef Menasheh ve'Efrayim: 'so the children of Joseph — Manasseh and Ephraim — received their inheritance.' The summary formula precedes the detailed descriptions. The order Manasseh-Ephraim here reverses the order in which Jacob blessed them (Genesis 48:14-20 — Ephraim first, Manasseh second) — a reminder that Jacob deliberately elevated Ephraim over Manasseh. Ephraim will indeed be the dominant tribe of the northern kingdom ('Ephraim' becomes a synonym for the northern kingdom in prophetic literature), while Manasseh's territory was divided between two sides of the Jordan.
5: gevul bnei Efrayim lemishpechotam: 'the border of the children of Ephraim by their clans.' Ephraim's territory occupies the central highlands south of Manasseh — the most important agricultural zone in the northern territories. The hills of Ephraim are lower and more accessible than the northern highlands, with numerous springs and wadis supporting intensive agriculture. Shiloh — the tabernacle site during the period of the judges (Joshua 18:1; 1 Sam 1-4) — lies within Ephraim's territory, making this tribe the host of the central sanctuary for the pre-monarchic period.
6: veyatza hagvul yamah el-Michmeta mitzafon: 'the border goes out westward to Michmethath on the north.' The boundary description of Ephraim is brief compared to Judah's — the tribe's territory is smaller and the boundaries simpler. Michmethath (possibly 'hiding place' or 'ambush') was on the border between Ephraim and Manasseh. The verb yatza (goes out, extends) and yafne (turns) are the standard boundary-description verbs, showing a consistent administrative vocabulary throughout the allotment chapters.
7: veyarad miYanoach Atarot uNa'arat: 'it goes down from Janoah to Ataroth and Naarath.' The eastern boundary of Ephraim descends toward the Jordan valley. Ataroth appears also in the Gad-Reuben territory (Numbers 32:3,34), suggesting a common name for multiple settlements. The eastern descent of Ephraim's territory toward the Jordan created the boundary with Benjamin below and Manasseh above — a complex three-way territorial junction managed through careful boundary delimitation.
8: miyTappuach yeled hagvul yamah nachal Qanah: 'from Tappuah the border goes westward to the brook Kanah.' Nachal Qanah (Brook Kanah, possibly 'brook of reeds') is a significant wadI on the western boundary of Ephraim, ultimately draining to the Mediterranean. The wadi serves as both a natural boundary and a water source — the dry-season stream bed provides a visible demarcation. The mention of Tappuah (apple/quince-tree town) as a border town connects to the detailed account of Manasseh's border with Tappuah in Joshua 17:7-8.
9: ve'haarym hamivdalot libnei Efrayim betoch nachalat bnei Menasheh: 'and the cities that were set apart for the children of Ephraim within the inheritance of Manasseh.' The arrangement of Ephraim having separate cities within Manasseh's territory — enclave settlements across tribal boundaries — is an unusual administrative feature reflecting the political complexity of the Joseph tribes. The enclave cities (all with their villages) reflect the intermingling of the two tribes in actual settlement patterns as opposed to the ideal territorial boundary.
10: velo-gershu et-haKena'ani hayoshev beGazer (garash, H1644; lo-garshu, H3808+1644): 'they did not drive out the Canaanites who lived in Gezer.' Lo-gershu (they did not drive out) is the chapter's key term and the beginning of a pattern that will characterize the entire allocation of the northern tribes: partial obedience in the face of fortified Canaanite cities. Gezer was one of the most heavily fortified cities in the Shephelah — a mighty Canaanite stronghold that remained Canaanite until Solomon's time, when Egypt conquered it and gave it to Solomon as a dowry (1 Kings 9:16). mas oved (mas, H4522; oved, H5647): 'forced labor/corvée' — the Canaanites were not expelled but enslaved as laborers, a tolerated compromise that violated YHWH's explicit command (Deuteronomy 7:2).
